commands.review_summary:
The branch imports Codex native-owned dynamic tool names, enables native Code Mode when explicit `toolsAllow` contains one of them, and adds a unit test for those allowlists.

PR surface: Source +6, Tests +30. Total +36 across 2 files.

Reproducibility: yes. source inspection gives a high-confidence path: current main returns false for explicit non-wildcard native `toolsAllow` entries, while the PR returns true for entries like `read`. I did not run a live cron or agent job in this read-only review.
commands.review_followup: Needs maintainer security/product review because the remaining choice is whether explicit native tool allowlists mean all native Code Mode tools or a separately enforced subset.

target.title: fix #86423: codex: explicit toolsAllow lists strip native read/write/edit/exec instead of enabling them
target.body:
## Summary

Fixed a data-loss bug where native codex tool surfaces were incorrectly disabled when `toolsAllow` explicitly listed native tool names (e.g. `read`, `write`, `edit`, `exec`).

**Root cause**: `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` only enabled the native surface when `toolsAllow` was `undefined` or contained the `"*"` wildcard. When users provided an explicit list of native tools, the native surface stayed disabled, making those tools unavailable.

**Fix**: Extended the logic so that `toolsAllow` also enables the native surface when it contains any tool name listed in `CODEX_APP_SERVER_OWNED_DYNAMIC_TOOL_EXCLUDES`. A `readonly string[]` cast was added to resolve a TS2345 type error where `normalizeCodexDynamicToolName` returns `string` but the `as const` array's `includes()` expects the narrow union type.

---

## Real behavior proof

- **Behavior or issue addressed:** When a cron job or agent session explicitly sets `toolsAllow: ["read","write","edit","memory_search"]`, native tools (read/write/edit/exec) are no longer silently dropped and the agent can actually perform file writes.

- **Real environment tested:** openclaw/openclaw repo, branch `feat/issue-86423`, build and type checks passing, new unit test added.

- **Exact steps or command run after this patch:**
  1. Modified `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.ts`: imported `CODEX_APP_SERVER_OWNED_DYNAMIC_TOOL_EXCLUDES` and updated `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface`
  2. Added test case in `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts` covering the new logic
  3. Ran `pnpm tsgo:extensions:test` — type checks pass
  4. Pushed to fork branch, CI checks all green (check-test-types, check-prod-types, check-additional-extension-package-boundary all pass)

- **Evidence after fix:**
  ```text
  $ pnpm exec vitest run extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts
   ✓ enables Codex native tool surfaces when toolsAllow explicitly includes native-owned tools
   ✓ disables Codex native tool surfaces when the effective exec target is node (existing)
  ```

- **Observed result after fix:** Native-owned tool names (`read`, `write`, `exec`, `bash`, `apply-patch`) correctly trigger native surface enablement; non-native tools (`message`, `memory_search`, `web_search`) keep it disabled as expected.

- **What was not tested:** Did not run a full cron job chain in production, but unit tests cover the core decision function. Edge cases of the dynamic tool filtering logic are covered by existing tests.

- **Target test file:** `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`

- **Root cause:** `shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` only treated `undefined` or `"*"` wildcards as enabling conditions. Explicit allowlists were treated as "narrow" even when they contained native tool names, disabling the entire native surface. This caused the nightly cron issue where writes silently failed while run status reported `ok`.

---

## Regression Test Plan

- **Target test file:** `extensions/codex/src/app-server/run-attempt.test.ts`
- **New test name:** "enables Codex native tool surfaces when toolsAllow explicitly includes native-owned tools"
- **Scenario locked in:** Verifies that `toolsAllow` lists containing native-owned tool names enable the native surface, while non-native-only lists keep it disabled
- **Why this is the smallest reliable guardrail:** Directly tests the decision function with the exact inputs from the issue

---

## Root Cause

`shouldEnableCodexAppServerNativeToolSurface` only treated `toolsAllow` being `undefined` or containing `"*"` as enabling conditions. Explicit allowlists were treated as "narrow" even when they contained native tool names, disabling the entire native surface. This caused nightly cron jobs using explicit allowlists to silently lose write capabilities while run status reported `ok`.

The fix detects whether `toolsAllow` contains any tool name from `CODEX_APP_SERVER_OWNED_DYNAMIC_TOOL_EXCLUDES` and enables the native surface accordingly, while maintaining strict control for non-native-only lists.
